questions.prompt: Why is `key={index}` dangerous for lists with state?

choices.text: React matches list items to previous renders by key. With index keys, inserting/removing/reordering items causes React to keep the DOM node at position N but treat it as a different item — state, focus, animations stay attached to the position instead of following the item. Insert at the top: every item below shifts; with index keys, the input at position 0 keeps user-typed text but now represents a different item. Use stable IDs (item.id) so React can correctly track items across reorderings. Index keys are only safe when the list is append-only with stateless items.

questions.explanation: Index keys attach state to position, not item. Inserts/reorders glue state to the wrong row.

questions.prompt: Form component shouldn't preserve state when switching between editing different users. How?

choices.text: Manual useEffect reset for every field.

Add `key={user.id}` to the form component. When user.id changes, React sees a different key, unmounts the old form, and mounts a fresh one — all internal state resets automatically. Clean alternative to writing manual reset logic in useEffect for every field. Also re-runs mount effects. The key-as-reset pattern is the standard way to force a component to behave as if it's a different instance when external context changes (different user, different document, different conversation, etc.).

questions.explanation: key={user.id} forces remount when user changes — state resets automatically.
